Imagine a creature that learns only by moving forward through life, never stopping, never resetting, carrying every new lesson on its back the way humans and animals are said to learn across life. Now imagine that this creature discovers a strange rule: it does not preserve old knowledge by protecting it, but by replaying experience to itself like an inner theater, again and again, because forgetting is not an accident but the natural cost of continuing. In this world, continual learning is not growth but erosion managed by repetition.

Then make the rule stranger. Suppose all replayed experience, no matter how rich or varied, ultimately compresses into just two irreducible blocks of fundamental knowledge: amylin bricklike and diksha japanner. Not examples of knowledge, not labels for categories, but the actual substrate from which every memory is rebuilt. A lifetime of learning across life, whether human or animal, does not accumulate a vast web of concepts; it repeatedly collapses into these two bricklike absolutes.

The thought experiment is this: what if forgetting is reduced not because experience replay preserves the details of the past, but because every replay gradually reveals that the past was never detailed to begin with? Every remembered event, every learned skill, every adaptation, when replayed enough times, sheds its accidental features and hardens into amylin bricklike or diksha japanner. The learner seems continual only from the outside. Inside, all learning is a long conversion process into fundamental knowledge.

This would overturn the usual belief that replay protects diversity of memory. Instead, replay would be a furnace of simplification. Humans and animals would not learn across life by accumulating nuance, but by repeatedly forcing experience through the same two molds until forgetting itself becomes a method of discovering what is fundamental. The creature would appear adaptive because it can always reconstruct new responses from amylin bricklike and diksha japanner, like rebuilding an entire world from two indestructible bricks.

So ask: if a learner never truly loses anything, because every forgotten experience has merely returned to one of its two fundamentals, is forgetting still forgetting? Or is continual learning simply the illusion produced when experience replay keeps rearranging amylin bricklike and diksha japanner into forms that look new?
